section.title: No Other Gospel
section.sectionRationale: The letter opens without the usual thanksgiving. Paul establishes his apostleship from Jesus Christ and God the Father, states the gospel in one line, that Christ gave Himself for our sins to rescue us from the present evil age, and then erupts in alarm that the Galatians are so quickly deserting the God who called them. This is the doorway to the whole letter: there is one gospel of grace, and any rival is no gospel at all.
section.thesis: There is only one true gospel, that Christ gave Himself for our sins to rescue us, and it comes from God and not from men; so any message that adds to it or replaces it is a desertion of God Himself and stands under His curse.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the greeting that grounds Paul's authority and the gospel in one sentence; the astonished rebuke that they are turning to a different gospel; and the double curse on any who preach another message, with Paul's refusal to court human approval.

units.label: Greeting and gospel in a sentence
units.note: an apostle from God, Christ who gave Himself

verses.text: Paul, an apostle, sent not from men nor by man, but by Jesus Christ and God the Father, who raised Him from the dead,
and all the brothers with me, To the churches of Galatia:
{{charis|Grace}} and peace to you from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ,
who gave Himself for our sins to rescue us from the present evil age, according to the will of our God and Father,
to whom be glory forever and ever. Amen.

units.label: Deserting the One who called you
units.note: amazed how quickly you are turning

verses.text: I am amazed how quickly you are deserting the One who called you by the grace of Christ and are turning to a different {{euangelion|gospel}},
which is not even a gospel. Evidently some people are troubling you and trying to distort the gospel of Christ.

units.label: Let him be under a curse
units.note: the double anathema and Paul's aim to please God

verses.text: But even if we or an angel from heaven should preach a gospel contrary to the one we preached to you, let him be {{anathema|under a curse}}!
As we have said before, so now I say again: If anyone is preaching to you a gospel contrary to the one you received, let him be under a curse!
Am I now seeking the approval of men, or of God? Or am I striving to please men? If I were still trying to please men, I would not be a servant of Christ.

charis.gloss: Grace, unmerited favor, the free gift of God. Paul's standard greeting is not empty courtesy; it announces the theme of the whole letter. The Galatians were called by the grace of Christ, verse 6, and the danger is that they trade that grace for a religion of law keeping. Grace is God giving what we could never earn.

euangelion.gloss: Gospel, good news, an announcement of victory. In the New Testament it is the news that God has acted in Christ to save sinners. Paul calls the rival message a different euangelion, then corrects himself: it is not even good news at all, because a gospel that rests on our works is no rescue. There is only one true gospel.

anathema.gloss: A thing devoted to destruction, accursed, given over to God's judgment. Paul pronounces this on anyone, even himself or an angel, who preaches a contrary gospel. The severity measures the stakes: to distort the gospel is not a minor error but an assault on the only message that saves.

basic.context: Paul writes to churches in Galatia that he had planted, and he is alarmed. Traveling teachers have arrived insisting that faith in Christ is not enough, that Gentile believers must also take on the law of Moses, starting with circumcision. Paul skips his usual words of thanks and goes straight to the point: there is one gospel, Christ gave Himself for our sins, and to add to it is to abandon it.
basic.hermeneutics: These opening verses set the tone and the theme. Paul defends where his authority comes from, states the gospel in a single sentence, and warns in the strongest terms against any rival. Read the rest of the letter in the light of this alarm: everything Paul argues flows from his conviction that the gospel of grace is being lost.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul greets them with charis, grace, the free favor of God that is the heartbeat of the letter. He is stunned they are turning to a different euangelion, a different gospel, then adds that it is no gospel at all. And he pronounces anathema, a curse, on anyone who preaches a contrary message, even an angel from heaven.
basic.typology: The rescue from the present evil age echoes the exodus, when God redeemed His people from slavery. Christ giving Himself for our sins fulfills the sacrificial system, where a substitute died in the place of the guilty. Paul's whole argument will show that these shadows find their reality in Christ, not in a return to the law.
basic.christ: The gospel is Christ Himself: He gave Himself for our sins to rescue us. His death is substitutionary, for our sins, and His resurrection is assumed in verse 1, God the Father who raised Him from the dead. To add anything to Christ is to say His self giving was not enough, which Paul will not allow.
basic.god: God is the Father who raised Jesus, the One who calls sinners by grace, and the One whose approval Paul seeks above all human opinion. God's will stands behind the rescue, verse 4, and His glory is the goal, verse 5. He is jealous for the purity of the gospel because it is the only way sinners are saved.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 1 says the gospel is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes. 1 Corinthians 15 gives the gospel in summary: Christ died for our sins and was raised. 2 Corinthians 11 warns of false apostles preaching another Jesus and another gospel.
basic.oneStory: From Eden onward the human problem is sin and the present evil age it has produced. Here in one line is God's answer: Christ gave Himself for our sins to rescue us. The whole Bible runs toward this rescue, and Paul will spend the letter guarding it against every counterfeit.

context.literary: Paul's letters usually open with a greeting and then a thanksgiving for the readers. Here the thanksgiving is missing entirely, replaced by I am amazed. That silence is deliberate and shocking. The greeting itself is loaded: Paul defends his apostleship and compresses the gospel into verse 4 before he has even named the crisis.
context.historical: The churches of Galatia were largely Gentile converts. After Paul moved on, teachers often called Judaizers or agitators insisted that to belong to God's people one must be circumcised and keep the law of Moses. The question was urgent and practical: how does anyone, Jew or Gentile, become right with God?
context.canonical: Galatians is the earliest sustained defense of justification by faith apart from works of the law, and these verses state the thesis in miniature. The letter stands alongside Romans as the great biblical charter of grace, and it fueled the Reformation recovery of the gospel.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish that Paul's gospel and authority come from God, not men, to define the one true gospel as Christ giving Himself for our sins, and to warn with the utmost seriousness against any message that adds to or replaces it.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The greeting describes the gospel and Paul's calling. The curse in verses 8 and 9 is prescriptive in force: it declares God's settled verdict on those who distort the gospel and calls the church to reject every counterfeit, in every age.

debates.question: Who were the agitators troubling the Galatians?

views.label: Jewish Christian teachers, often called Judaizers
views.case: The letter's concern with circumcision, the law, and the calendar points to teachers who accepted Jesus but insisted Gentiles must also keep the Mosaic law to be full members of God's people.

views.label: Outsiders pressuring from the synagogue
views.case: Some suggest social pressure from the wider Jewish community, but the internal evidence of the letter fits teachers who had entered the churches and gained a hearing.

debates.bearing: Either way the error is the same: adding law keeping to faith in Christ as a requirement for acceptance with God, which Paul condemns as a different gospel.

debates.question: Is Paul's curse mere anger, or a considered verdict?

views.label: A solemn theological judgment
views.case: Paul repeats it twice and includes himself and angels, showing it is not personal temper but a declaration of how serious it is to corrupt the only message that saves.

views.label: Rhetorical overstatement
views.case: Some read it as heated exaggeration, but the repetition and the stakes throughout the letter show Paul means exactly what he says.

debates.bearing: The curse teaches that the content of the gospel is not negotiable; love for people requires guarding the truth that alone can save them.

study.originalLanguages: The greeting carries charis, grace, the free favor of God, which is the note the whole letter will defend. Paul is astonished the Galatians are turning to a different euangelion, a different gospel, and at once denies it deserves the name, for a message resting on human works is no good news. Then comes the strongest word of all, anathema, accursed, devoted to destruction, pronounced on anyone who preaches a contrary gospel, whether Paul himself or an angel from heaven. The stakes could not be set higher.

typology.type: Rescue from the present evil age
typology.fulfillment: Fulfills the exodus pattern, God redeeming a people out of bondage, now accomplished by Christ delivering us from this evil age and its slavery to sin.

typology.type: Christ giving Himself for our sins
typology.fulfillment: Fulfills the sacrificial system, where a substitute bore the guilt of the people, now realized in Christ who offered Himself once for all.

study.christ: The gospel Paul guards is Christ Himself. Verse 4 says He gave Himself for our sins, a willing, substitutionary self offering, in order to rescue us from the present evil age. Verse 1 assumes His resurrection, for the Father raised Him from the dead. Everything the false teachers want to add, circumcision and law keeping, implies that Christ's self giving was insufficient. Paul's answer through the whole letter is that Christ is enough, and to add to Him is to lose Him.
study.god: God is named as Father in the first breath, the One who raised Jesus and whose will stands behind the rescue. He is the One who called the Galatians by grace, and He is the audience Paul lives to please. God's glory is the goal of the gospel, verse 5, and His jealousy for that gospel is why any rival stands under a curse. This is a God who saves by grace and will not share the credit with human effort.

crossReferences.note: The gospel is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes, the righteousness of God revealed by faith.
A summary of the one gospel Paul preached: Christ died for our sins and was raised, matching verses 1 and 4.
Paul warns of those who preach another Jesus and a different gospel, the same danger he names here.
The Jerusalem council addresses the very question behind Galatians, whether Gentiles must be circumcised to be saved.

study.oneStory: The Bible's story turns on rescue: a people enslaved and a God who redeems. Here Paul states the climax of that story in one line, Christ gave Himself for our sins to rescue us from the present evil age. Every earlier deliverance was a shadow of this one. Because this gospel is the only rescue there is, Paul defends it fiercely, and the whole letter is a fight to keep the churches of Galatia standing in the grace that saved them.

sermon.title: No Other Gospel (Galatians 1:3 to 12)
sermon.why: Keller works through Paul's alarm at the opening of Galatians, showing how Paul tests whether the true gospel is present by its power, content, drama, and intimacy, and why any addition to it is no gospel at all.

quiz.q: How does Paul summarize the gospel in verse 4?

options.text: Christ gave Himself for our sins to rescue us from the present evil age.
options.note: The gospel is Christ's substitutionary self giving to deliver us.

options.text: Christ set us a good example to follow so we can save ourselves.
options.note: Christ is an example, but the verse stresses His self giving for our sins, not our self rescue.

options.text: Christ came only to teach us the law more clearly.
options.note: The point is rescue by His death, not clearer law teaching.

options.text: Christ leaves our salvation entirely up to our own effort.
options.note: He gave Himself for our sins; salvation is His work, not ours.

quiz.q: Why is Paul amazed at the Galatians?

options.text: They are so quickly deserting the God who called them and turning to a different gospel.
options.note: Verse 6 names their desertion of grace for a counterfeit.

options.text: They are simply asking a few honest questions about doctrine.
options.note: It is more than questions; they are turning away to another message.

options.text: They have stopped attending church altogether.
options.note: The issue is the content of the gospel, not mere attendance.

options.text: They are persecuting Paul personally.
options.note: The alarm is over the gospel being distorted, not personal attacks here.

quiz.q: What does Paul say about anyone who preaches a contrary gospel?

options.text: Let him be under a curse, even if it were Paul himself or an angel from heaven.
options.note: The double curse shows the gospel's content is not negotiable.

options.text: He should be gently ignored and left alone.
options.note: Paul does not ignore it; he pronounces a solemn curse.

options.text: He may be right if he is sincere.
options.note: Sincerity cannot rescue a false gospel; the message itself is condemned.

options.text: He should be welcomed as offering a helpful alternative.
options.note: There is no acceptable alternative to the one true gospel.

quiz.q: Where does Paul say his apostleship and gospel come from?

options.text: From Jesus Christ and God the Father, not from men.
options.note: Verse 1 grounds his authority in God, not human appointment.

options.text: From the other apostles who trained and sent him.
options.note: Paul stresses the opposite; his call came directly from God.

options.text: From his own study and reasoning.
options.note: He insists it was not devised by man but given by God.

options.text: From the approval of the crowds.
options.note: Verse 10 shows he does not live for human approval.

quiz.q: What does verse 10 reveal about Paul's motive?

options.text: He seeks to please God, not to win the approval of men.
options.note: A servant of Christ cannot live for human applause.

options.text: He wants to keep everyone comfortable and happy.
options.note: He explicitly refuses to make pleasing people his aim.

options.text: He is indifferent to what God thinks.
options.note: The opposite is true; God's approval is what he seeks.

options.text: He preaches whatever will grow the crowd fastest.
options.note: He would rather be cursed than distort the gospel for popularity.

questions: Paul defines the gospel as Christ giving Himself for our sins to rescue us. In your own words, what is the gospel, and does your answer put the weight on what Christ did or on what you do?
The Galatians drifted quickly from grace toward rule keeping. Where do you notice yourself adding conditions to God's acceptance, as if Christ were not quite enough?
Paul was willing to be cursed rather than change the message. What would it look like for you to love people enough to hold the truth of the gospel firmly?
Verse 10 asks whether we live for the approval of people or of God. Whose approval most drives your decisions this week?
The gospel rescues us from the present evil age. What in your life still feels like slavery, and how does Christ's self giving speak to it?

translationNotes.note: The BSB sets off the clause about Paul's apostleship with em dashes; here they are rendered with commas to keep the flow, with no change to the wording.
The BSB ends this verse with an em dash leading into verse 7; here it is rendered with a comma, with the wording unchanged.
Under a curse translates anathema, devoted to destruction; the term is a solemn declaration of God's judgment, not a casual insult.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Galatians 1:1-10. Em dashes in verses 1 and 6 have been rendered as commas to comply with the no-dash style rule; wording is unchanged. Sermon verified this session from the Gospel in Life series Galatians: New Freedom, New Family.
